claim: ESPEN 2021 practical guideline directs evaluation of nutritional intake, weight change, and BMI from cancer diagnosis onward.
effect_size_or_number_with_n_N: 43 recommendations; strong recommendation, very-low evidence
source: Muscaritoli et al., Clin Nutr (ESPEN practical guideline)
exact_quotable_line_le_25_words: regularly evaluate nutritional intake, weight change, and body mass index (BMI), beginning with cancer diagnosis

claim: ASPEN 2024 recommends validated nutrition screening before treatment initiation and regularly thereafter, with defined tool thresholds.
effect_size_or_number_with_n_N: Thresholds: MNA ≤23.5, MST ≥2, MUST ≥1/≥2, NRS-2002 ≥2/≥3, NUTRISCORE ≥5, PG-SGA-SF ≥7/≥8; 20/738 studies
exact_quotable_line_le_25_words: recommended before treatment initiation and regularly thereafter, depending on treatment course.

claim: ASCO 2020 states clinicians may refer patients with advanced cancer and appetite or weight loss to a registered dietitian.
effect_size_or_number_with_n_N: Low evidence, moderate strength of recommendation
population: Advanced cancer with anorexia and/or weight loss
exact_quotable_line_le_25_words: Clinicians may refer patients with advanced cancer and loss of appetite and/or body weight to a registered dietitian

claim: International consensus set weight loss greater than 5% as the agreed diagnostic criterion for cancer cachexia.
effect_size_or_number_with_n_N: >5% (or >2% with BMI<20 or reduced muscle mass)
source: Fearon et al., Lancet Oncol (cachexia consensus)
exact_quotable_line_le_25_words: The agreed diagnostic criterion for cachexia was weight loss greater than 5%

claim: A 2025 colorectal RCT found individualized nutrition improved chemotherapy tolerance and QOL and reduced adverse effects.
effect_size_or_number_with_n_N: n=88: dose-intensity reduction 9.30% vs 25.89% (p=.02); regimen change 25.58% vs 53.33% (p<.01); delay 13.95% vs 35.56% (p=.019); cycle-8 physical function 72.71 vs 57.63
source: Wang et al., Nutrition and Cancer
exact_quotable_line_le_25_words: Individualized nutritional interventions improved chemotherapy tolerance and QOL and reduced adverse effects in this patient cohort.

claim: Oral nutritional supplements after colorectal surgery significantly reduced chemotherapy modifications and sarcopenia.
effect_size_or_number_with_n_N: 212 analyzed: chemo modification 21.2% vs 36.8% (p=.024); sarcopenia 28.6% vs 42.1% (p=.040); no weight/QoL effect
exact_quotable_line_le_25_words: chemotherapy modifications, such as delay, dose reduction, or termination, were significantly reduced in the ONS group

claim: Nutritional counseling in metastatic colorectal cancer improved weight but not the proportion with clinically relevant muscle loss.
effect_size_or_number_with_n_N: n=107: weight +1.7 kg (p=.045); muscle loss 30% vs 31% (p=.467); PFS/OS magnitudes UNVERIFIED
source: van der Werf et al., Clin Nutr
exact_quotable_line_le_25_words: The proportion of patients with a clinically relevant decrease in skeletal muscle area of ≥6.0 cm2 did not differ

claim: A high-protein supplementation meta-analysis found less body-weight loss but no health-related quality-of-life difference.
effect_size_or_number_with_n_N: 35 RCTs, n=3701: weight MD +1.45 kg (95% CI 0.42–2.48, I²=80%); no HRQoL difference
exact_quotable_line_le_25_words: Patients who received high-protein supplementation lost less body weight than controls

claim: Evidence gap: no verified RCT restricted to colon-cancer/FOLFOX patients with ≥5% loss reporting interruptions, dose reductions, weight, QoL, and survival together.
effect_size_or_number_with_n_N: UNVERIFIED
population: Colon cancer / FOLFOX with ≥5% weight loss
exact_quotable_line_le_25_words: UNVERIFIED

claim: KDIGO 2024 Practice Point 4.1.3 advises reviewing and limiting OTC medicines and remedies that may harm people with CKD.
effect_size_or_number_with_n_N: Practice Point 4.1.3 (no numeric estimate)
exact_quotable_line_le_25_words: Review and limit the use of over-the-counter medicines and dietary or herbal remedies that may be harmful for people with CKD.

claim: A 2020 joint guideline (grade 1C) advises avoiding NSAIDs in moderate CKD when receiving ACE inhibitors, ARBs, or diuretics.
effect_size_or_number_with_n_N: Recommendation grade 1C
exact_quotable_line_le_25_words: patients with moderate CKD (eGFR 30–59 mL/min/1.73 m2) receiving ACE inhibitors, ARBs or diuretics.

claim: Triple therapy (NSAID + ACEi/ARB + diuretic) raised acute kidney injury risk, greatest at the start of treatment.
exact_quotable_line_le_25_words: The risk was greatest at the start of treatment.

claim: In lab-defined AKI, triple therapy raised risk with a number-needed-to-harm near 158 per treatment-year.
effect_size_or_number_with_n_N: n=78,379: triple RR 1.64 (1.25–2.14); NNH 158 vs >300 for dual
exact_quotable_line_le_25_words: numbers needed to harm for 1 year treatment with NSAID of 158 vs. over 300

claim: The claim that KDIGO forbids every NSAID at every eGFR 30–60 is unverified and must not be asserted.
effect_size_or_number_with_n_N: UNVERIFIED
exact_quotable_line_le_25_words: UNVERIFIED

claim: A drug-drug-interaction meta-analysis found physicians overrode about 90% of alerts.
effect_size_or_number_with_n_N: 570,776 prescriptions: override 90% (95% CI 85.6–95.0, I²=100%)
source: Felisberto et al., Health Informatics J
exact_quotable_line_le_25_words: the overall prevalence of alert override by physicians was 90%

claim: An appropriateness review reported wide ranges in both override rates and override appropriateness.
effect_size_or_number_with_n_N: 23 studies: override 46.2%–96.2%; appropriateness 29.4%–100%
source: Poly et al., JMIR Med Inform
exact_quotable_line_le_25_words: The range of average override alerts was 46.2% to 96.2%.

claim: Severity-tiered alerting was associated with higher DDI-alert compliance in the inpatient setting.
effect_size_or_number_with_n_N: 71,350 alerts: compliance 29% vs 10%; most-severe acceptance 100% vs 34%
exact_quotable_line_le_25_words: Tiered alerting by severity was associated with higher compliance rates of DDI alerts in the inpatient setting

claim: A cluster-RCT found accountable justification and peer comparison reduced inappropriate antibiotic prescribing.
effect_size_or_number_with_n_N: 248 clinicians/47 practices: accountable justification DID −7.0 pts (−9.1 to −2.9); peer comparison DID −5.2 pts
exact_quotable_line_le_25_words: Mean antibiotic prescribing rates decreased from 23.2% at intervention start to 5.2% at intervention month 18 for accountable justification.

claim: A cluster-RCT found a clinician-facing nudge increased statin prescribing while a patient-only nudge did not.
effect_size_or_number_with_n_N: Clinician nudge +5.5 pts; combined +7.2 pts
exact_quotable_line_le_25_words: The patient nudge alone did not change statin prescribing relative to usual care

claim: A simulation found modal alerts far more effective than nonmodal, supporting reserving modals for high severity.
effect_size_or_number_with_n_N: 720 tasks: modal OR 11.56 vs no alert
exact_quotable_line_le_25_words: modal alerts were over three times more effective than nonmodal alerts.

claim: Physicians averaged just over 16 minutes of EHR time per encounter, a third of it chart review.
effect_size_or_number_with_n_N: ~100M encounters, 155k physicians: 16 min 14 s/encounter; chart review 33%, documentation 24%, ordering 17%
exact_quotable_line_le_25_words: Physicians spent an average of 16 minutes and 14 seconds per encounter using EHRs

claim: Physicians spent nearly six hours of an 11.4-hour workday interacting with the EHR.
effect_size_or_number_with_n_N: n=142, 3 years: 5.9 of 11.4 h; clerical/admin 44.2%; inbox 23.7%
source: Arndt et al., Ann Fam Med
exact_quotable_line_le_25_words: physicians spend more than one-half of their workday, nearly 6 hours, interacting with the EHR

claim: Across four EHR systems the same tasks showed roughly ninefold time and eightfold click differences.
effect_size_or_number_with_n_N: 4 systems: ~9-fold time difference; ~8-fold click difference
exact_quotable_line_le_25_words: an average of a nine-fold difference in time and eightfold difference in clicks

claim: Higher patient-message volume was associated with more daily EHR time and more chart switches.
effect_size_or_number_with_n_N: 3,996 clinicians: +25.5 min EHR time; +18.1 chart switches daily
exact_quotable_line_le_25_words: associated with a 25.5 min increase in EHR time

claim: Greater conversational multitasking was associated with increased EHR time and more patient switches.
effect_size_or_number_with_n_N: 3,232 clinicians: ≥4 concurrent conversations ≈ +54.8 min, +41.6 switches; trainees +82.3 min
exact_quotable_line_le_25_words: Higher levels of conversational multitasking were associated with increased EHR time and more patient switches

claim: In a small study an AI system saved first-time physician users 18% of task time without changing accuracy.
effect_size_or_number_with_n_N: n=12: 18% time saved (10.5 vs 12.8 min); accuracy no significant difference
source: Chi et al., JAMA Netw Open
exact_quotable_line_le_25_words: the AI system saved first-time physician users 18% of the time

claim: ICU chart review took a median of 9.2 minutes across about 26.5 screens.
effect_size_or_number_with_n_N: median 9.2 min (IQR 7.3–14.7); 26.5 screens
source: Nolan et al., Appl Clin Inform
exact_quotable_line_le_25_words: ICU review median 9.2 min (IQR 7.3–14.7), 26.5 screens

claim: eConsults averaged about 15 minutes of specialist time and avoided most in-person visits.
effect_size_or_number_with_n_N: 15.4 min specialist time; 1.4-day response; 62% avoided in-person consult
source: Nunavut eConsult, Int J Circumpolar Health
exact_quotable_line_le_25_words: eConsults took an average of 15.4 minutes of specialist time to complete

claim: In a Spanish oncology coding study, reimbursement per admission rose about 719 euros when malnutrition was coded.
effect_size_or_number_with_n_N: n=266: +719.32€ per admission (Spain, euros; price-year UNVERIFIED)
exact_quotable_line_le_25_words: The cost reimbursement for each admission increased an average of 719.32€.

claim: Medicare cancer patients with malnutrition incurred more than double the mean emergency-department claim costs.
effect_size_or_number_with_n_N: >2.8M claims: ED cost $10,724 vs $4,935 (2018 USD)
source: Sulo et al., J Nutr Health Aging
exact_quotable_line_le_25_words: such patients incurred more than 2-fold higher mean ED claim costs

claim: AKI was associated with about $1,795 adjusted incremental hospitalization cost and roughly one extra day.
effect_size_or_number_with_n_N: 29.76M hospitalizations: adjusted incremental $1,795 ($1,692–$1,899, 2012 basis), +1.1 days; dialysis-AKI +$11,016, +3.9 days
source: Silver et al., J Hosp Med
exact_quotable_line_le_25_words: AKI is associated with excess hospitalization costs and prolonged LOS.

claim: AKI after coronary procedures added about $8,416 in index cost and an estimated $1.67 billion national burden.
effect_size_or_number_with_n_N: n=2.76M: index +$8,416; excess burden $1.67 billion (dollar-year UNVERIFIED)
source: Prasad et al., Cathet Cardiovasc Interv
exact_quotable_line_le_25_words: Overall excess healthcare burden associated with AKI was $1.67 billion.

claim: A modeled nutrition-economics analysis estimated $6.23/day in-hospital nutrition cost and a modeled (not observed) saving.
effect_size_or_number_with_n_N: 27 trials, n=6,803: $6.23/day; modeled saving $2,818/patient (MODELED, not observed)
exact_quotable_line_le_25_words: The estimated daily per-patient cost for in-hospital nutrition was US$6.23.

claim: A nonrandomized home-health QI reported about $1,500 per-patient savings from reduced 90-day utilization.
effect_size_or_number_with_n_N: Program cost $261/patient; total savings $2,318,894 (~$1,500/patient); mixed 2013/2016/2017 dollars
exact_quotable_line_le_25_words: Total cost savings from reduced 90-day healthcare resource utilization was $2,318,894, or $1500 per patient treated.

claim: A VA study estimated the average cost of a patient no-show at $196 in 2008.
effect_size_or_number_with_n_N: $196 per no-show (2008 USD)
source: Kheirkhah et al., BMC Health Serv Res
exact_quotable_line_le_25_words: The average cost of no-show per patient was $196 in 2008.

claim: A palliative-clinic reminder QI cut no-shows and estimated about $79,200 in annual opportunity savings.
effect_size_or_number_with_n_N: No-show 11.8%→6.9%; ~$79,200 annual savings (2016 USD, opportunity basis)
source: Lagman et al., Am J Hosp Palliat Med
exact_quotable_line_le_25_words: equivalent to an annual savings of about $79,200.
